![Fulcrum Digital Logo](https://fulcrumdigital.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/08/fd-logo-2021-1.svg "Fulcrum Digital Logo")
![Fulcrum Digital Logo](https://fulcrumdigital.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/08/fd-logo-2021-1.svg)

# Autonomous Systems

![](https://fulcrumdigital.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/anatomy-autonomous-systems-glossary-fulcrum-digital3.png "anatomy-autonomous-systems-glossary-fulcrum-digital3")

Autonomous systems are self-operating technologies that are capable of making decisions and executing actions without human intervention. Powered by AI, sensors, and real-time data, these systems adapt to dynamic conditions, optimizing workflows across industries like manufacturing, finance, and logistics.

## **Detailed Definition & Explanation**

[Autonomous systems](https://fulcrumdigital.com/platform/ryze/) are intelligent, self-directed systems that can perform complex tasks, make real-time decisions, and operate independently within defined parameters. They rely on a combination of [artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning,](https://fulcrumdigital.com/services/ai-ml/) sensor data, and control logic to function in environments that are either structured (e.g., factories) or dynamic (e.g., customer service workflows).

These systems typically include:

Unlike traditional automation, which follows rigid scripts, autonomous systems can adapt to changes, learn from interactions, and even coordinate with other systems. In [Agentic AI](https://fulcrumdigital.com/platform/ryze/) environments, multiple autonomous agents collaborate, self-optimize, and act toward shared enterprise goals, whether it’s managing inventory, routing claims, or analyzing risk.

![](https://fulcrumdigital.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/anatomy-autonomous-systems-glossary-fulcrum-digital1.png)

## **Types of Autonomous Systems in Agentic AI**

In the context of Agentic AI, autonomous systems are purpose-built to act with minimal supervision and high contextual awareness. They can be categorized based on task complexity, domain of operation, and degree of coordination:

## **Why It Matters**

The value of autonomous systems goes far beyond efficiency. Understanding why they matter helps clarify not just their function, but their strategic importance across the enterprise.

![](https://fulcrumdigital.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/anatomy-autonomous-systems-glossary-fulcrum-digital2-1024x538.png)

## **Real-World Examples**

**FD Ryze**

[FD Ryze](https://fulcrumdigital.com/platform/ryze/) is an autonomous [Agentic AI platform](https://fulcrumdigital.com/platform/ryze/) that deploys hyper-autonomous agents to streamline operations across business functions. Designed to be private, language model agnostic, and orchestrated, these agents act independently, adapt in real time, and collaborate to solve complex enterprise tasks, such as claims triage, IT incident routing, or supply chain optimization.

**AutoGPT**

AutoGPT is an open-source autonomous agent that uses [large language models](https://fulcrumdigital.com/services/ai-ml/generative-ai/) (LLMs) to break down high-level goals into sub-tasks and execute them iteratively. It simulates multi-step reasoning and autonomous decision-making, offering a glimpse into the early capabilities of self-directed AI systems.

**AutoGen (Microsoft Research)**

AutoGen is a [multi-agent orchestration framework](https://fulcrumdigital.com/platform/ryze/) that enables language agents to collaborate on complex tasks. Each agent is assigned a specialized role, and they communicate via natural language to delegate, solve, and validate work—a powerful demonstration of agentic coordination in action.

**Devin AI (Cognition Labs)**

Devin is an AI software engineer capable of independently coding, debugging, and executing software tasks. It can interpret user goals, set its own objectives, and iterate until a solution is reached. This is a compelling application of Agentic AI in the [software development lifecycle](https://fulcrumdigital.com/platform/ryze/).

# Ultimate Guide to Autonomous Systems

## What Are Autonomous Systems?

![What Are Autonomous Systems?](https://images.blackberry.com/is/image/blackberry/autonomous-systems-it1-pillar-what-is-it-m-1?wid=656&fmt=png-alpha)

## Definition

An autonomous system is one that can achieve a given set of goals in a changing environment—gathering information about the environment and working for an extended period of time without human control or intervention. Driverless cars and autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) used in warehouses are two common examples.

Autonomy requires that the system be able to do the following:

## An autonomous system can sense, perceive, plan and act without intervention.

## Autonomous vs. Semi-Autonomous Systems

The vast majority of current systems known as autonomous are semi-autonomous rather than fully autonomous. Cars with driver support systems such as lane keep assist and advanced braking systems are semi-autonomous, as are robotic surgery systems, robot vacuums and most unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs and drones). Most fully autonomous systems, such as driverless cars, remain too costly, data-intensive, power-consuming or unsafe for widespread public use.

Significant progress is being made. Electric vehicles, autonomous vehicle perception systems and connected car platforms have passed Gartner’s "peak of inflated expectations" and through the "trough of disillusionment." These systems have reached the "slope of enlightenment" in the [Connected Vehicle and Smart Mobility Hype Cycle](https://www.wevolver.com/article/following.the.autonomous.vehicle.hype).

## Examples of Autonomous Systems

Video of the first communication in Canada between an autonomous vehicle and live city infrastructure on a public road.

## Autonomous Vehicles

Both passenger and commercial vehicles include autonomous capabilities, which exist on a continuum, as identified by the six levels of driving automation defined by [SAE J3016](https://www.sae.org/news/2019/01/sae-updates-j3016-automated-driving-graphic):

Many passenger vehicles in widespread use meet SAE J3016 Level 0, 1 or 2.

![The levels of driving automation in SAE J3016 range from alerts to full autonomy.](https://images.blackberry.com/is/image/blackberry/autonomous-systems-it2-pillar-figure-1?wid=1688&fmt=png-alpha)

## Autonomous vehicles will account for 15 percent of global light vehicle sales by 2030.

Source: [McKinsey-Automotive revolution—perspective towards 2030. January 2016](https://www.mckinsey.com/industries/automotive-and-assembly/our-insights/disruptive-trends-that-will-transform-the-auto-industry)

As for autonomous commercial vehicles, while many fewer trucks are built annually than cars, autonomous trucks will radically change cargo transport and the use of public highways. One autonomous cargo transport technique is known as truck platooning. In truck platooning, a human driver in a tractor-trailer truck leads a convoy of autonomous tractor-trailer trucks, enabling a single driver to move much more cargo. Automated driver support systems allow each autonomous truck to follow the truck in front of it at a set distance. The human driver can drop off the follower trucks and pick up new ones at predetermined locations along a highway.

Autonomous vehicles also include those that use rail. Autonomous freight trains will move cargo with a human acting as an observer.

## Autonomous Robots

Otto the autonomous snowplow is an autonomous robot developed at L-SPARK, Canada's largest SaaS accelerator, with advice and software from BlackBerry QNX.

In agriculture, the idea of fully autonomous tractors has engaged generations of farmers, but currently autonomous tractors require a supervising operator. Other autonomous systems used on farms include automatic milking machines and strawberry-picking robots.

In the medical field, robots assist surgeons in performing high-precision procedures, such as coronary artery bypass and cancer removal.

High-mobility autonomous systems, such as four-legged walking robots, can navigate obstacles, access difficult-to-reach locations and perform tasks hazardous to humans. Examples include industrial inspection robots and rescue mission robots.

## Autonomous Warehouse and Factory Systems

## Autonomous Drones

Unmanned aerial vehicles, known as UAVs or drones, are small self-piloting autonomous aircraft. Drones have long been used for reconnaissance, surveying, asset inspection and environmental studies. Two common uses for drones are agriculture and oil well inspection.

Autonomous UAV technology has its basis in the autopilot technology used by passenger airplane captains in commercial aircraft. Fully autonomous (pilotless) passenger aviation remains a distant goal. Similarly, un-crewed UAVs are usually piloted remotely rather than directing their own flight paths. In UAVs, two tasks commonly performed by fully autonomous systems are airborne refueling and ground-based battery switching.

## Sensors and Sensor Fusion

## Sensors Used in Autonomous Systems

Sensors help an autonomous system determine its location, identify informational signs, and avoid obstacles and other hazards. The following describes common types of sensors and their purposes and explains how the use of multiple sensors can overcome a weakness of one type of sensor by pairing it with another one with different strengths and weaknesses.

## Unlike image sensors and LiDAR, radar sensors are unaffected by weather conditions.

Radar sensors are often paired with cameras to achieve image quality that is closer to the high-resolution images provided by LiDAR.

How Radar and LiDAR Compare:

| Not applicable | Radar | Rotating LiDAR |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Source of name | Radio detection and ranging | Light detection and ranging |
| Types of waves | Radio waves | Light waves from laser beams |
| When invented | 1930s—detect hostile aircraft | 1960s—Apollo moon missions |
| Image quality | May lack detail to identify objects | 3D monochromatic image |
| Spatial resolution | Even high-resolution radar is much lower resolution than LiDAR | Good at detecting small objects, such as the hand signals of a bicyclist |
| Distance | Longer, allowing more time for reaction | Shorter, 30-200 meters |
| Reliability | More reliable due to solid state components | Less reliable due to more moving parts |
| Velocity | Accurately determines relative traffic speed | Poor detection of the speed of other vehicles |
| Field of view | Loses sight around curves | 360° view and can track direction of movement |
| Object identification | Imprecise. May confuse multiple smaller objects as one larger object. | Precise. Can detect which direction a pedestrian is facing. |
| Effect of weather | Unaffected by snow, fog, rain and dusty wind | Impaired by snow, fog, rain and dusty wind |
| Power requirements | Less power | More power |
| Price | $50-200 | $7,500 |
| Size | Small | Large |
| Best uses | Low-speed vehicles, such as an autonomous plow, and car parking assistance systems | Vehicles that travel at higher speeds |

###### Table 1: Advantages and disadvantages of radar and LiDAR.

## Wireless Vehicle Communications

In addition to sensors on an autonomous system, machines in the environment can also provide information to the system. Autonomous vehicles will pass information to nearby systems and vice versa. Vehicle-to-everything (V2X or V2E) communication between a vehicle and its environment improves safety by enabling communication with the following types of systems:

Such communication is achieved with one of two technologies: 5G or dedicated short-range communication (DSRC) using the IEEE Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments (WAVE) 802.11p standard. Current LTE cellular technology does not allow for peer-to-peer communication which is necessary for V2V or V2P. In the future, 5G vehicle-to-everything communication (C-V2X) will make cars smarter and driving safer.

## Sensor Fusion

![Sensor fusion integrates overlapping information from multiple types of sensors for greater accuracy and effectiveness](https://images.blackberry.com/is/image/blackberry/autonomous-systems-it2-figure-2?wid=1688&fmt=jpg)

## Sensor fusion combines data from multiple types of sensors to produce a more accurate picture of the environment.

To perceive and understand the sensor data, the system must filter disparate data sources, localize data assets, interpret those assets and classify data. Sensor fusion algorithms—such as the Kalman filter, Bayesian networks and convolutional neural networks—help the autonomous system ensure that it is working to extract the maximum possible information from the sensor data.

## Software for Autonomous Systems

Building safe, secure and reliable autonomous systems begins with the architecture, then the design, and then the software foundation. In this section, you’ll learn about autonomous systems architecture, functional safety systems, the future of autonomous system software, a safe software foundation and the many security considerations for autonomous systems.

The growth of autonomous systems will involve a transition from
hardware-driven equipment to software-driven electronics, regardless of
whether the autonomous system is a snowplow or a surgical system. The
software requirements for real-time control, safety, security and
reliability are similar across industries. At BlackBerry QNX, we provide
the operating system or software foundation for developers facing the
myriad challenges of building safe, secure and reliable software for use
in a wide variety of autonomous systems.

## Autonomous System Software Architecture

![An example platform architecture for an autonomous vehicle.](https://images.blackberry.com/is/image/blackberry/autonomous-systems-it2-pillar-figure-3?wid=1688&fmt=png-alpha)

## Functional Safety Systems

Unsafe autonomous systems are a grave concern of consumers, governments and industry watchers. It may not be fair, but autonomous systems are held to a higher standard of safe behavior than humans. That is, they must prove a better safety record than humans or they will not be accepted by the public.

In passenger vehicles, safety features such as lane-departure warnings and collision detection, and active-control features such as automatic braking and lane-keep assist, must always work. In comparison, the public is willing to accept mistakes from human drivers.

## People are willing to accept that humans make mistakes, but autonomous systems are held to a higher standard.

## Abstraction

Autonomous systems today are propriety networks of systems from multiple suppliers integrated by a system developer, which makes it difficult for developers to scale software and limits the availability of third-party after-market applications.

In the future, developers will interact with autonomous systems via a software platform that abstracts the hardware, abstracts the sensors and pushes the interface to a higher-level set of software services via an application programming interface (API). This will free developers from having to interact with a specific type of LiDAR, radar or camera in use—and enable them to simply request information via high-level services provisioned with APIs. Connectivity will be similarly abstracted, so the communications within the car, with other vehicles and with the cloud will be simpler for a developer to implement without requiring a deep understanding of a specific communication technology.

## In the future, a developer will call an API to get information from a car, without needing to understand the underlying hardware.

## Software Foundation for Safe Systems

At the core of any safety-critical autonomous system is a safe, reliable and secure software foundation or operating system. The following components provide the building blocks for safe autonomous systems.

### Autonomous Vehicle Architectures from BlackBerry QNX

## How BlackBerry QNX Can Help You

## BlackBerry QNX Software

From surgical robots and cars to trains and traffic lights, BlackBerry® QNX® software provides the foundation for safe, reliable and secure systems:

## The QNX Neutrino RTOS is used in hundreds of millions of systems, including driverless vehicles and autonomous robots.

## BlackBerry QNX Professional Services

Developing safe, reliable and secure software for mission-critical and safety-critical applications of all kinds is a core competency at BlackBerry QNX. We offer training, consulting, custom development, root cause analysis and troubleshooting, system-level optimization and onsite services for developers of a variety of embedded systems in automotive, medical, robotics, industrial automation, defense and aerospace, among other industries.

Learn more about our [professional services](/en/services), including [safety services](/en/services/safety-services).

## Industry Leadership

The BlackBerry QNX [Autonomous Vehicle Innovation Centre](/en/resource-center/qnx-autonomous-vehicle-innovation-centre) (AVIC) helps developers of software for connected and self-driving vehicles through production-ready software developed independently and in collaboration with partners in the private and public sectors.

Additionally, as part of a program with [L-SPARK](https://www.l-spark.com/), Canada’s largest software-as-a-service accelerator, BlackBerry QNX helps companies research and develop product prototypes in areas such as robotics, device security, sensor fusion (e.g. LiDAR, radar, cameras and GPS), functional safety, analytics, medical devices and autonomous vehicles.

The Vation Ventures Glossary
----------------------------

Autonomous Systems: Definition, Explanation, and Use Cases
==========================================================

Autonomous systems, a cornerstone of artificial intelligence, are systems capable of performing tasks with minimal human intervention. These systems, which can range from self-driving cars to intelligent chatbots, are designed to operate independently, making decisions based on their programming and the data they collect. This article delves into the definition, explanation, and use cases of autonomous systems, providing a comprehensive understanding of this critical aspect of artificial intelligence.

Autonomous systems are not a new concept, but their application and sophistication have grown exponentially with the advancement of technology. They are now ubiquitous, found in various sectors, including transportation, healthcare, manufacturing, and more. As we delve deeper into the world of autonomous systems, it's essential to understand their definition, how they work, and their real-world applications.

Definition of Autonomous Systems
--------------------------------

Autonomous systems, in the context of artificial intelligence, are systems that can operate and perform tasks without direct human control. They are designed to make decisions based on their programming and the data they collect, allowing them to adapt to different situations and perform tasks that would otherwise require human intervention.

These systems can range from simple automated machines, such as vending machines, to complex systems like autonomous vehicles and intelligent robots. Regardless of their complexity, all autonomous systems share the common characteristic of being able to function independently, without the need for constant human supervision or control.

### Key Characteristics of Autonomous Systems

While autonomous systems can vary greatly in their complexity and functionality, they all share certain key characteristics. These include the ability to perceive their environment, the ability to make decisions based on this perception, and the ability to act on these decisions to achieve their goals.

Perception involves collecting data about the environment, which can be done through various means, such as sensors, cameras, or other data collection devices. Decision-making involves processing this data and making decisions based on it. This can involve complex algorithms and machine learning techniques. Finally, action involves executing the decisions made, which can involve physically manipulating the environment or sending signals to other systems.

### Types of Autonomous Systems

Autonomous systems can be categorized into several types based on their functionality and the level of autonomy they possess. These include fully autonomous systems, semi-autonomous systems, and supervised autonomous systems.

Fully autonomous systems can operate independently without any human intervention. They are capable of making decisions and taking actions based on their programming and the data they collect. Semi-autonomous systems, on the other hand, require some level of human intervention. They can perform certain tasks independently, but require human input for more complex decisions or tasks. Supervised autonomous systems operate under the constant supervision of a human operator, who can intervene if necessary.

Explanation of Autonomous Systems
---------------------------------

Understanding how autonomous systems work requires a basic understanding of artificial intelligence and machine learning. These systems are designed to mimic certain aspects of human intelligence, such as the ability to learn from experience, adapt to new situations, understand complex concepts, and make decisions based on incomplete or uncertain information.

At the heart of an autonomous system is its decision-making process. This involves collecting data, processing it, making decisions based on the processed data, and then acting on these decisions. This process is typically carried out by a combination of hardware and software, with the software being responsible for the decision-making process.

### Data Collection and Processing

Data collection is a critical aspect of autonomous systems. These systems rely on data to make decisions, and the quality and quantity of this data can significantly impact their performance. Data can be collected through various means, such as sensors, cameras, or other data collection devices.

Once the data is collected, it needs to be processed. This involves cleaning the data, removing any irrelevant or erroneous data, and transforming it into a format that can be used by the decision-making algorithms. This process can involve complex data processing techniques and algorithms.

### Decision Making and Action

The decision-making process in autonomous systems involves using the processed data to make decisions. This can involve complex algorithms and machine learning techniques. The decisions made by the system can range from simple actions, such as turning on a light, to complex decisions, such as navigating a self-driving car through traffic.

Once a decision has been made, the system needs to act on it. This can involve physically manipulating the environment, such as a robot moving an object, or sending signals to other systems, such as a traffic management system adjusting traffic lights based on traffic conditions.

Use Cases of Autonomous Systems
-------------------------------

Autonomous systems have a wide range of applications, from simple tasks such as controlling home appliances to complex tasks such as driving cars and managing traffic. The following sections will explore some of the most common use cases of autonomous systems.

It's important to note that while these use cases represent some of the most common applications of autonomous systems, they are by no means exhaustive. The potential applications of these systems are vast and continue to grow as technology advances.

### Autonomous Vehicles

One of the most well-known applications of autonomous systems is in the field of autonomous vehicles. These vehicles are designed to navigate and operate without human intervention, using a combination of sensors, cameras, and advanced algorithms to perceive their environment and make decisions.

Autonomous vehicles have the potential to significantly improve road safety by reducing human error, which is a leading cause of accidents. They can also improve efficiency by optimizing routes and reducing traffic congestion.

### Industrial Automation

Autonomous systems are also widely used in industrial automation. These systems can perform a wide range of tasks, from simple tasks such as sorting products to complex tasks such as assembling intricate machinery.

By automating these tasks, companies can improve efficiency, reduce costs, and increase productivity. Additionally, autonomous systems can perform tasks that are dangerous or difficult for humans, improving worker safety.

### Healthcare

Autonomous systems are increasingly being used in healthcare, from robotic surgery to patient monitoring. These systems can perform tasks with a level of precision and consistency that is difficult for humans to achieve, improving patient outcomes and reducing the risk of complications.

For example, autonomous systems can assist surgeons in performing complex surgeries, providing them with precise control and reducing the risk of human error. They can also monitor patients' vital signs and alert healthcare professionals if any abnormalities are detected.

Conclusion
----------

Autonomous systems, with their ability to operate independently and make decisions based on data, are a critical aspect of artificial intelligence. They have a wide range of applications, from autonomous vehicles to industrial automation and healthcare, and their potential continues to grow as technology advances.

Understanding the definition, explanation, and use cases of autonomous systems is crucial for anyone interested in artificial intelligence. As these systems become more prevalent, their impact on our lives will only continue to grow.

SUMMARY:
In contrast, the autonomous systems have the capability of recognizing different situations and making a decision accordingly without human interaction. Autonomous systems including Autonomous Vehicles (AV) operate with or without human intervention. Autonomous systems perform intelligent functions by perceiving the environment and implementing responsive actions based on sensor observations and subsequent inference [38]. In absence of an onboard human operator, a question arises on how to meet the self-health monitoring needs (proprioceptive capabilities) of the emerging generation of autonomous systems in order to ensure their safe and reliable operation. However, in order to do so, a paradigm shift from the design and development of conventional ISHM systems is necessary to accommodate the intelligence requirements for the health management of future autonomous aerospace vehicles and adaptive systems operating in highly interconnected and information-rich environments.

### 1.1.1.2 Autonomous System

1.
:   Definition

    The [autonomous system](/topics/mathematics/autonomous-system "Learn more about autonomous system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") means no external input system (Khalil, 2002). The autonomous system is described as a form of differential equations , *x*(*t*0) = *x*0*t* ∈ [*t*0,∞), where *x* is the [system state](/topics/mathematics/system-state "Learn more about system state from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), *f* is the [nonlinear function](/topics/mathematics/nonlinear-function "Learn more about nonlinear function from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with *x* and *t*, *t* is time.

2.
:   Example

    For example, there is a bicycle there; suddenly, the bicycle fell down by a gust of wind, the wind is the external input, but the bike has also been affected by gravity; it is not entirely without external input, so there is no external input is a relative concept (Fig. 1.3).

    (Therefore, the autonomous system is a relative concept.)

## [22 European Symposium on Computer Aided Process Engineering](/science/article/pii/B9780444595201500026)

[2012, Computer Aided Chemical Engineering](/bookseries/computer-aided-chemical-engineering)M. Rodríguez, ... R. Sanz

### 1 Introduction

Autonomous systems refer to systems capable of operating in a real world environment without any form of external control for extended periods of time. Reasons to provide systems with autonomy range from cost reduction to improved performance and [dependability](/topics/engineering/dependability "Learn more about dependability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Moreover, managing the increasing complexity of these systems has turned into delegating their configuration, optimisation, and repair to the systems themselves. In the process industry systems are more complex every day because they are highly interrelated and integrated, not only process operation units but the control and external applications (including the software that supports them). The use of a ontology on an existing system has clear benefits (as reuse, reasoning, documentation, etc.) but what is missing is a way to engineer that complex system to comply with a certain set of requirements, in this case including autonomy. The ontology presented in this paper addresses both tasks at the same time, the engineering and the description of the system. The remaining of the paper is structured as follows: section 2 presents the requirements of the ontology, sections 3 and 4 describe the ontology and its methodology, section 5 shows an application and, finally, section 6 draws conclusions.

## [Autonomy and metrics of autonomy](/science/article/pii/S1367578820300274)

[2020, Annual Reviews in Control](/journal/annual-reviews-in-control)Panos Antsaklis

### Abstract

The quest for autonomy has been a pervasive theme in human culture through-out history. In this paper a general definition of [autonomous systems](/topics/mathematics/autonomous-system "Learn more about autonomous systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is presented and discussed that leads naturally to the establishment of metrics to measure the level of autonomy of a system. This definition is based on the systems ability to achieve goals under uncertainties and it does not involve the means by which the goals are achieved, such as sensing and feedback. This paper takes the point of view that any [autonomous system](/topics/mathematics/autonomous-system "Learn more about autonomous system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a [control system](/topics/mathematics/control-system "Learn more about control system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and that to achieve higher levels of autonomy one may need to add methods traditionally developed in areas such as operations research and AI. The work presented here is based on earlier work by the author on functional architectures for autonomous spacecrafts.

## [Current advancements on autonomous mission planning and management systems: An AUV and UAV perspective](/science/article/pii/S1367578818300257)

[2018, Annual Reviews in Control](/journal/annual-reviews-in-control)Adham Atyabi, ... Samia Nefti-Meziani

### 3 Autonomy vs. automation

There is a big difference between two concepts of automatic and autonomous operations. In automatic systems, the machine exactly follows the programmed commands, and it has no choice for making decisions. In contrast, the [autonomous systems](/topics/computer-science/autonomous-system "Learn more about autonomous systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") have the capability of recognizing different situations and making a decision accordingly without [human interaction](/topics/computer-science/human-interaction "Learn more about human interaction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Hence, developing more intelligent autonomous systems with the ability to run autonomously, reconfiguring based on varying situations, and efficient mission planning according to new circumstances, is one of the significant fields of interest for many system designers (Johnson, Patron, & Lane, 2007; MahmoudZadeh & BairamZadeh, 2018).

Automatic systems play an essential role in the modern flight control systems and have potential in terms of increasing efficiency, comfort, and safety of the motion, but they should not be mistaken with autonomous systems. Stenger et al. (2007) provided the following explanation to distinguish between the two categories: “*an automatic system is designed to fulfill a pre-programmed task. It cannot place its actions into the context of its environment and decide between different options. An autonomous system, on the other hand, can select amongst multiple possible action sequences in order to achieve its goals. The decision which action to choose is based on the current knowledge, that is, the current internal and external situation together with internally defined criteria and rules.*”

Autonomous systems can also be seen and explained in the context of cognitive systems. Vernon (2011) explained the relationship between cognition and autonomy as “*One position is that cognition is the process by which an autonomous self-governing agent acts effectively in the world in which it is embedded. As such, the dual purpose of cognition is to increase the agent's repertoire of effective actions and its power to anticipate the need for future actions and their outcomes*”.

Following these principles, an [autonomic system](/topics/engineering/autonomic-nervous-system "Learn more about autonomic system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") must be capable of sensing, monitoring, and understanding of all its [operational context](/topics/computer-science/operational-context "Learn more about operational context from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and probable environmental events. It should adapt to the variable uncertain environments with minimum human involvement. This process is strictly tightened to the definition of SA proposed by Endsley (1995). An autonomous vehicle should also be aware of its [internal state](/topics/computer-science/internal-state "Learn more about internal state from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and capabilities to assess whether current mission goal is reachable or find an alternative solution.

## [Intelligent Energy Management for Environmental Monitoring Systems](/science/article/pii/B978012809859200005X)

[2017, Smart Sensors Networks](/book/9780128098592/smart-sensors-networks)Petr Musilek, ... Tomáš Bartoň

### Autonomous harvesting systems

Autonomous harvesting systems fully satisfy their energy needs from ambient sources, without [batteries](/topics/engineering/battery-electrochemical-energy-engineering "Learn more about batteries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Pirapaharan et al., 2012; Yi et al., 2008). Autonomous systems can only operate when the energy source is available, but their lifetime and performance are not limited by storage inefficiencies (e.g. [round trip](/topics/engineering/roundtrip "Learn more about round trip from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") efficiency, self-discharge and aging). These systems are inherently governed by the so-called energy neutrality principle since they can never consume more energy than their harvesting device can deliver (Kansal et al., 2007).

It is a [photovoltaic system](/topics/engineering/photovoltaic-system "Learn more about photovoltaic system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") completely independent of any other energy source; It supplies the user with electricity without being connected to the electric grid. In most cases, autonomous systems require [batteries](/topics/engineering/battery-electrochemical-energy-engineering "Learn more about batteries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to store energy. They are typically used to power homes in remote locations, on islands, in mountains, and are used for applications such as remote monitoring and water pumping [22].

We can see on the horizon a new age of autonomous systems in which the opportunities for applications are endless: self-driving cars and trucks, autonomous package and cargo delivery systems, police and military operations, earth exploration, critical infrastructure inspection and maintenance, and medical diagnostics and operating room robotics, to name a few. Autonomous systems with machine reasoning and intelligence will forever change how machines serve humans. ## [Sensor Technology for Autonomous Vehicles](/science/article/pii/B9780128225486001229)

[2023, Encyclopedia of Sensors and Biosensors](/referencework/9780128225493/encyclopedia-of-sensors-and-biosensors)Henry Alexander Ignatious, ... Manzoor Ahmed Khan

### Abstract

Autonomous systems including Autonomous Vehicles (AV) operate with or without human intervention. To achieve partial (or) full-fledged autonomy, the AVs must be able to sense and react to their surrounding environment. Autonomous vehicles (AVs) use a variety of sensor technologies to observe their surroundings and make logical decisions based on the data gathered, just as humans do. Perception systems (sensors onboard AVs) provide enough information to enable autonomous transportation and mobility under [optimal operating conditions](/topics/engineering/optimal-operating-condition "Learn more about optimal operating conditions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Comprehensive sensing of the environment requires multiple sensors to operate simultaneously to acquire precise information from the surroundings and the internal state of the vehicle. In reality, there are still a number of challenges that might obstruct the operation of AV sensors and, as a result, reduce their effectiveness under more realistic situations. This chapter discusses various features and facts related to physical AV sensors, including the categories of sensors, their functionality, characteristics, behavior toward climate conditions, and their applications.

## [Materials design for robotic platforms enabling unique mechanisms of projectile protection](/science/article/pii/B9780081022603000196)

[2019, Robotic Systems and Autonomous Platforms](/book/9780081022603/robotic-systems-and-autonomous-platforms)Pingwei Liu, ... Michael S. Strano

### Abstract

As autonomous, [robotic systems](/topics/engineering/robotic-system "Learn more about robotic systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") assume an increasingly diverse number of form factors, sizes, and shapes, fundamentally new capabilities become possible. Applications involving passive materials or systems can become active, or redesigned entirely to enable new paradigms. This chapter highlights this concept in the area of projectile protection. Traditionally the domain of heavy, static materials, positioned on the target and designed to dissipate energy in as short a stopping distance as possible, armor and protective materials can assume different forms entirely when associated with highly mobile, autonomous, possibly robotic systems that move the function of protection off target. Such systems can use the mobility of autonomous systems to dynamically place themselves along the trajectory at an intermediate location between launch and target of an incident projectile. In this chapter, we explore an alternative protective strategy whereby the protective material exists as a free-body, i.e., an autonomous system, along the projectile trajectory distinct from and at a stand-off distance from, the actual target. We have employed the shorthand description for this concept *Ambient Armor*, to convey the idea that armor may be a system or network of objects orbiting or traveling along specific paths near the target and through the projectile path. The exploration of this concept takes the form of an approximate modeling framework highlighting new material opportunities that exist for protective systems in this space.

## [Advances in Integrated System Health Management for mission-essential and safety-critical aerospace applications](/science/article/pii/S0376042121000622)

[2022, Progress in Aerospace Sciences](/journal/progress-in-aerospace-sciences)Kavindu Ranasinghe, ... Kathiravan Thangavel

### 1.3 ISHM systems in trusted autonomous system applications

Autonomous systems perform intelligent functions by perceiving the environment and implementing responsive actions based on sensor observations and subsequent inference [38]. In the automotive domain, the push towards greater autonomy is driven by the rise of [Intelligent Transport](/topics/engineering/intelligent-transport "Learn more about Intelligent Transport from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") Systems (ITS). The advent of autonomous cars can be traced back to the 1980s, where the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA) funded the Autonomous Land-driven Vehicle (ALV) project demonstrated autonomous robotic control of a vehicle at moderate speeds based on [Light Detection and Ranging](/topics/engineering/light-detection-and-ranging "Learn more about Light Detection and Ranging from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (LiDAR) and computer vision [39]. In the early 2000s DARPA began to organize and sponsor competitions which invited robotic engineers to design autonomous cars capable of overcoming a specific challenge [40]. Moving on to the 2010s, the increasing interest in autonomous vehicle technology had persuaded many major automotive manufacturers such as Toyota, Mercedes-Benz, Volvo, GM and Ford to invest in driverless car systems. Several suppliers specialize in autonomous system technology, and most car manufacturers currently have prototype systems that are in various stages of development and testing [41,42].

In the aerospace domain, autonomy has recently gained significant traction in [Unmanned Aircraft Systems](/topics/engineering/unmanned-aircraft-system "Learn more about Unmanned Aircraft Systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (UAS) applications, with autonomous drones already being widely used for small-scale pre-scheduled missions such as building inspection [43]. More recently, larger scale autonomous aerial system applications such as urban air taxis [44] have been envisioned. However, despite the anticipated safety and efficiency benefits brought forward by such autonomous system applications, one of the major inhibitors to their widespread uptake is public trust [45,46].

One particular concern is regarding the reliability and integrity of autonomous systems in aerial, terrestrial and maritime transport. This has elicited new requirements and casted greater importance on [ISHM](/topics/engineering/integrated-system "Learn more about ISHM from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In absence of an onboard human operator, a question arises on how to meet the self-health monitoring needs (proprioceptive capabilities) of the emerging generation of autonomous systems in order to ensure their safe and reliable operation. This is particularly important in network-centric frameworks such as [UAS](/topics/engineering/unmanned-aircraft-system "Learn more about UAS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") Traffic Management (UTM), which consists of fully interoperable and interdependent airborne and ground-based systems, so that an unforeseen airborne system failure event or any anomalous behaviour could have adverse implications on other airborne and ground agents in the network. When autonomous systems advance to more complicated and long-term tasks such as delivery, infrastructure inspection and surveillance, they will need to integrate information about the existing state of their system capabilities and resources into their mission and task planning. The solution lies in the field of IHMM systems that recognize patterns and irregularities in sensor readings to diagnose current faults and predict future failures and make subsequent adjustments to the mission profile to greatly improve the probability of mission success. The safety-critical role that IHMM will play in autonomous aerospace system applications is discussed further in Section 7.

Moreover, within the two categories of TAS, i.e., ACP and CPH systems, it is important to consider the impact that IHMM has on both the human operator and system itself. The interactions between these two elements is modulated to meet specific goals.

Table 2 shows the functional allocation between automated systems and the human operator at 10 Levels Of Automation (LOA) based on Endsley and Kaber's taxonomy [47]. Four functions are considered in assessing the LOA: monitoring (detection of visual indicators, audible scanning, etc.), generating options (developing strategies to achieve goals or resolve issues), selecting which options to take and lastly, implementing the chosen options. A majority of current ISHM techniques (see Table 1) can be considered to be at LOA 5 Decision Support at most as their primary responsibilities are the monitoring of subsystem parameters for [performance degradation](/topics/engineering/performance-degradation "Learn more about performance degradation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and assisting the human operators or maintenance personnel with health information to take the necessary steps to avoid a failure. However, as autonomous system operations grow in size and scope, the number of functions performed autonomously will reduce the reliance on human operators for tactical decision-making. It is evident that with increasing LOA, IHMM systems will have more responsibility in generating and selecting options in response to detected faults or impending performance degradation. This level of responsibility can only be trusted to an IHMM system that exhibits high levels of accuracy and integrity, which in turn can only be developed using AI-based reasoning techniques. It is clear that the increasing levels of autonomy result in the human having a less significant role to play. However, the design of an IHMM system should still take into account the optimal methods of Human-Machine Interactions (HMI), particularly when presenting useful health information and implementing strategies in response to faults [48].

Table 2. Levels of Automation as defined by Endsley and Kaber [47].

| Level of Automation (LOA) | Roles | | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Monitoring | Generating | Selecting | Implementing |
| (1) Manual control | Human | Human | Human | Human |
| (2) Action support | Human/Computer | Human | Human | Human/Computer |
| (3) Batch processing | Human/Computer | Human | Human | Computer |
| (4) Shared control | Human/Computer | Human/Computer | Human | Human/Computer |
| (5) Decision support | Human/Computer | Human/Computer | Human | Computer |
| (6) Blended decision making | Human/Computer | Human/Computer | Human/computer | Computer |
| (7) Rigid system | Human/Computer | Computer | Human | Computer |
| (8) Automated decision making | Human/Computer | Human/Computer | Computer | Computer |
| (9) Supervisory control | Human/Computer | Computer | Computer | Computer |
| (10) Full automation | Computer | Computer | Computer | Computer |

Advances in the field of trusted autonomous vehicles bring forward new technologies such as Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) technology and intra-vehicular communication channels that integrate both digital and physical components to enable different CPH systems to communicate and cooperate in real-time with each other and with humans. This connectivity will support the dispatch of safety-related information (including health status generated from IHMM systems), which would be instrumental in maximising the safety of all road users. The exchange of health data to maximise safety crucially applies to aerospace vehicles and systems as well. However, owing to the [interconnectivity](/topics/engineering/interconnectivity "Learn more about interconnectivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and novelty of these subsystems, assessing their state-of-health and performance would be more challenging as compared to traditional subsystems, yet equally as important due to their safety-criticality in trusted autonomous vehicles.

It is clear that IHMM will play a major role in supporting the ongoing evolution toward trusted autonomous systems. However, in order to do so, a paradigm shift from the design and development of conventional ISHM systems is necessary to accommodate the intelligence requirements for the health management of future autonomous aerospace vehicles and adaptive systems operating in highly interconnected and information-rich environments.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Autonomous systems are self-operating technologies capable of planning, executing tasks, and making decisions with minimal or no human intervention, utilizing AI and real-time data.
Independent operation enhances efficiency by reducing reliance on human oversight, allowing systems to adapt to dynamic environments and optimize processes across various applications.
Autonomous systems are revolutionizing industries such as transportation, manufacturing, healthcare, and logistics by improving safety, efficiency, and productivity while reducing operational costs.

Advancements in AI, machine learning, sensor technology, and data analytics are propelling the growth of autonomous systems, enabling them to perform complex tasks with greater accuracy and reliability.
This report aims to provide a comprehensive overview of autonomous systems software, examining definitions, advancements, industry impacts, and future perspectives to guide stakeholders.
